1: Estin de pistis elpizomenōn hypostasis, pragmatōn elenchos ou blepomenōn — "now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen" (hypostasis: substance, underlying reality, foundation — the same word as Hebrews 1:3 [exact imprint of his hypostasis] and Hebrews 3:14 [if we hold the beginning of our hypostasis]): in Greek philosophy hypostasis referred to the underlying reality beneath appearances; here faith is described as the hypostasis of the hoped-for things — not hope itself but the substantiality, the made-real quality, that faith gives to what is hoped for. Elenchos: proof, demonstration, conviction — a legal/logical term for evidence that compels belief: faith is the demonstration of unseen realities, not a subjective state of optimism.
3: Pistei noumen katērtisthai tous aiōnas rhēmati theou, eis to mē ek phainomenōn ta blepomena gegonenai — "by faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible" (katērtisthai tous aiōnas rhēmati theou: the ages were fitted-together/constituted by the word of God — katartizō: to prepare, put in order, constitute): eis to mē ek phainomenōn ta blepomena gegonenai: so that the visible came to be not from visible things — the creation-from-nothing principle (creatio ex nihilo): the visible order does not derive from pre-existing visible material but from the divine word that preceded and generated it.
6: chōris de pisteōs adynaton euarestēsai; pisteusai gar dei ton proserchomenon tō theō hoti estin kai tois ekzētousin auton misthapadotēs ginetai — "and without faith it is impossible to please him, for whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him" (adynaton euarestēsai: impossible to please — the third adynaton of Hebrews, establishing faith as the absolute prerequisite for approaching God; tois ekzētousin auton: to those who seek him — present active participle: the seeking is ongoing, not a one-time quest): misthapadotēs: rewarder, one who gives back wages — misthos + apodidōmi; a compound found only here in the NT: God repays the investment of faith-seeking with himself.
10: exedecheto gar tēn tous themelious echousan polin, hēs technitos kai dēmiourgos ho theos — "for he was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God" (technitos: craftsman, artisan — one who designs and makes; dēmiourgos: public craftsman, builder — in Plato the Demiurge is the craftsman-god who orders the cosmos): the city with foundations is the opposite of a tent (the patriarchal dwelling) — permanent, foundationed, divinely constructed. Abraham's tent-dwelling was not failure but faith — he lived in the type while seeking the antitype.
13: kata pistin apethanon houtoi pantes, mē labontes tas epangelias, alla pōrrothen autas idontes kai aspasamenoi kai homologēsantes hoti xenoi kai parepidēmoi eisin epi tēs gēs — "these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth" (pōrrothen autas idontes: having seen them from far off — the patriarchs' eschatological vision that reached beyond their earthly lifetime; aspasamenoi: having greeted, embraced — the gesture toward something at a distance: a wave, a salute): xenoi kai parepidēmoi: strangers and sojourners/exiles — the technical terms for resident-aliens in Hellenistic usage; the patriarchs self-identify as non-citizens of the present order.
24: Pistei Mōysēs megas genomenos ērnēsato legesthai huios thygatros Pharaō — "by faith Moses, when he was grown up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter" (ērnēsato legesthai: refused to be called — the double verb: refusing + the passive infinitive of naming; it was not a name he bore but a name given by others that he rejected): the faith-act is the refusal of a social identity. Egyptian royal adoption made Moses heir to immense privilege; his refusal is faith's voluntary social descent in solidarity with the covenant people.
26: meizona plouton hēgēsamenos tōn Aigyptou thēsaurōn ton oneidismon tou Christou — "he considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt" (meizona plouton hēgēsamenos: having considered greater wealth — the deliberate cognitive evaluation of comparative worth; ton oneidismon tou Christou: the reproach of Christ — the identification of Moses's suffering with the Messiah's reproach is the Christological reading of Psalms 69:9 and Hebrews 89:50-51): Moses's reproach is identified with Christ's because it was reproach suffered for the sake of the same covenant promise — a typological solidarity across the testaments.
33: hoi dia pisteōs katēgōnisanto basileias, eirgatēsanto dikaiosynēn, epetychon epangeliōn, ephraxan stomata leontōn — "who through faith conquered kingdoms, enforced justice, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions" (katēgōnisanto basileias: conquered kingdoms — the military-athletic verb of wrestling/competing to a finish; the OT judges and kings who won battles): epetychon epangeliōn (obtained promises): not the complete eschatological promise (Hebrews 11:39) but specific historical promises — Gideon's deliverance, David's kingship, etc. Ephraxan stomata leontōn: stopped the mouths of lions — Daniel in the lions' den (Dan 6) and Samson (Judges 14:6).
35: hoi de ou prosdexamenoi tēn apolutrōsin, hina kreittōnos anastaseōs tychōsin — "some were tortured, refusing to accept release, so that they might rise again to a better resurrection" (ou prosdexamenoi tēn apolutrōsin: not having accepted the release/redemption — the same apolutrōsis as 9:15 and Ephesians 1:14; here not the redemptive act but the offer of release from torture): kreittōnos anastaseōs: a better resurrection — not the resuscitation of 11:35a (returning to mortal life) but the eschatological resurrection to immortal life. The faith that refuses earthly rescue for the sake of the coming resurrection is the supreme expression of Hebrews 11:1's assurance-of-hoped-for-things.
39: kai houtoi pantes martyrēthentes dia tēs pisteōs ouk ekomisanto tēn epangelian — "and all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised" (martyrēthentes: having been witnessed to, testified about, approved — the passive of martyreō: to bear witness; God's testimony about them in Scripture is the divine commendation): ouk ekomisanto tēn epangelian (did not receive the promise — the singular promise): the entire OT community of faith died before the fulfillment; they received historical-provisional fulfillments but not the eschatological completion. Tou theou peri hēmōn kreitton ti problepsamenos (v40: God having provided something better for us) — the new covenant community is the completing context for the OT cloud of witnesses.
2: en tautē gar emarturēthēsan hoi presbyteroi — "for by this the people of old received their commendation" (emarturēthēsan: were borne witness to, testified about — aorist passive: God's testimony about them came from without, not from their own self-assessment): hoi presbyteroi: the elders, the ancestors — not ecclesiastical officers but the patriarchs and figures of Israel's past. En tautē (by this faith): the antecedent is the definition of faith in v1; the OT saints were commended precisely through this faith-as-substance-and-evidence. Scripture's testimony about them constitutes the divine commendation.
4: Pistei pleiona thysian Abel para Kain prosēnenken tō theō, di' hēs emarturēthē einai dikaios, martyrountos epi tois dōrois autou tou theou, kai di' autēs apothanōn eti lalei — "by faith Abel brought God a better offering than Cain. Through his faith he was commended as righteous, when God himself testified about his gifts. And by his faith Abel still speaks, even though he is dead" (pleiona thysian: a better/more/greater sacrifice — pleionos: the comparative of greatness, not specifying in what way): the text of Genesis does not explain why Abel's sacrifice was accepted; Hebrews supplies the answer: faith. Eti lalei: still speaks — present tense: Abel's voice continues through Scripture. The blood of Abel cried out from the ground (Genesis 4:10); the author of Hebrews reads that continuing cry as the ongoing testimony of faith.
5: Pistei Enōch metetethe tou mē idein thanaton, kai ouch heurisketo dioti metethēken auton ho theos — "by faith Enoch was taken from this life so that he did not experience death. He could not be found, because God had taken him away" (Genesis 5:24): metetethe: was translated/transferred — aorist passive of metatithēmi: to transfer from one place to another; the same verb used for the transferring of the priesthood in Hebrews 7:12. Tou mē idein thanaton (so as not to see death — genitive articular infinitive of result): the translation bypassed death entirely — a unique testimony to faith that pleased God. Pro gar tēs metathēseōs emarturēto euaresthēkenai tō theō (for before the translation he had been testified to as having pleased God — perfect infinitive: the pleasure stood as an established fact before the event).
7: Pistei Nōe chrēmatistheis peri tōn mēdepō blepomenōn eulabētheis kateskeuasen kibōton eis sōtērian tou oikou autou, di' hēs katekrineken ton kosmon kai tēs kata pistin dikaiosynēs egeneto klēronōmos — "by faith Noah, when warned about things not yet seen, in holy fear built an ark to save his family. By his faith he condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness that is in keeping with faith" (chrēmatistheis: having been divinely warned/instructed by oracle — chrēmatizō: to conduct business, to receive a divine response; same verb as Hebrews 8:5 [Moses instructed]): eulabētheis: moved with godly fear, reverently careful — eulabeia: the reverent-cautious posture of one who takes seriously the divine warning. Katekrineken ton kosmon: condemned the world — not by pronouncing judgment but by his actions: his building of the ark implicitly condemned those who did not build, as his salvation made their non-response inexcusable.
8: Pistei kaloumenos Abraam hypēkousen exelthein eis topon hon ēmellen lambanein eis klēronomian, kai exēlthen mē epistamenos pou erchetai — "by faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go to a place he would later receive as his inheritance; and he set out without knowing where he was going" (hypēkousen: obeyed — the root of hypakoē [obedience]: literally hearing-under, submissive hearing that issues in action): the grammar is precise: kaloumenos (present passive participle: while being called) + hypēkousen (aorist: he obeyed) + exēlthen (aorist: he went out) — the obedience and departure were simultaneous with the calling, not subsequent to deliberation. Mē epistamenos pou erchetai: not knowing where he was going — the faith-obedience precedes the understanding of destination.
9: Pistei paroikēsen eis gēn tēs epangelias hōs allotrian, en skēnais katoikēsas meta Isaak kai Iakōb tōn synklēronōmōn tēs epangelias tēs autēs — "by faith he made his home in the promised land like a stranger in a foreign country; he lived in tents, as did Isaac and Jacob, who were heirs with him of the same promise" (paroikēsen: sojourned — the word for the resident-alien who lives in a land without citizenship; paroikos was the technical status for a foreign resident): hōs allotrian (as though it were foreign): the promised land is possessed through faith-promise, not by legal title — the very land promised is inhabited as though it belonged to others. En skēnais katoikēsas: having dwelt in tents — the tent-dwelling is a sign of the non-possessive mode of the promise: the heir lives as a guest in his own inheritance until the promise is fulfilled.
11: Pistei kai autē Sarra steira dynamin eis katabolēn spermatos elaben kai para kairon hēlikias, epei piston hēgēsato ton epangeilamenos — "and by faith even Sarah, who was past the age, received the ability to conceive because she considered him who had made the promise to be faithful" (autē Sarra steira: Sarah herself being barren — the very one who laughed at the promise [Genesis 18:12] received what she doubted through faith): dynamin eis katabolēn spermatos: power/capacity for the laying-down of seed — katabolē: a throwing-down, founding; here the biological process of conception. Para kairon hēlikias: beyond the season of age, past the natural time — the impossibility is noted to anchor the miracle in biological fact. Epei piston hēgēsato: because she considered faithful — the faith-act is cognitive: an assessment of God's reliability.
12: dio kai aph' henos egennēthēsan, kai tauta nenenekrōmenou, kathōs ta astra tou ouranou tō plēthei kai hōs hē ammos hē para to cheilos tēs thalassēs hē anarithmētos — "and so from this one man, and he as good as dead, came descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and as countless as the sand on the seashore" (nenenekrōmenou: as good as dead — perfect passive participle of nekroō: having been made dead, rendered as dead; Abraham's reproductive capacity is described in the same terms as Isaac's future death-and-return [v19]): the double simile (stars of heaven + sand of the sea) draws on the covenant promises of Genesis 15:5 and Hebrews 22:17 — the very promises made to the one who was as good as dead. From biological death to astronomical and oceanic multitude: the contrast establishes the resurrection-logic of the covenant.
14: hoi gar toiauta legontes emphanizousin hoti patrida epizētousin — "people who say such things show that they are looking for a country of their own" (emphanizousin: they make manifest, they publicly show — emphanizō: to reveal, to disclose; the profession of stranger-and-sojourner status reveals the inner orientation): the public confession of being a stranger (xenos kai parepidēmos) is a declaration of loyalty: one does not confess homelessness unless one is looking for a home elsewhere. The self-designation as strangers to the present order is simultaneously a declaration of citizenship in the coming order.
15: kai ei men ekeinēs emnēmoneuon aph' hēs exēlthon, eichon an kairon anakampsai — "if they had been thinking of the country they had left, they would have had opportunity to return" (eichon an kairon anakampsai: they would have had an opportunity to return — second-class contrary-to-fact conditional): the argument from opportunity: those who left Mesopotamia were not prevented from returning; the roads were open, the option existed. The fact that they did not return shows the homeland they sought was not the one they left. Anakampsai: to return, to bend back — the image of turning one's path back to the point of origin.
16: nyn de kreittōnos oregnontai, tout' estin epouraniou; dio ouk epaischydnetai autōn ho theos theos epikaleisthai, hētoimasen gar autois polin — "instead, they were longing for a better country — a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared a city for them" (oregnontai: they long for, they stretch out for — oregomai: to reach out for, to desire with outstretched hand): ouk epaischydnetai ho theos theos epikaleisthai autōn: God is not ashamed to be called their God — the reversal of shame: the patriarchs bore the shame of homelessness; God bears the name of their God publicly and without shame. Hētoimasen polin: he has prepared a city — perfect: already done. The preparation preceded the heirs.
17: Pistei prosenenoken Abraam ton Isaak peirazomenos, kai ton monogenē prosepheren ho tas epangelias anadexamenos — "by faith Abraham, when God tested him, offered Isaac as a sacrifice. He who had received the promises was about to sacrifice his one and only son" (prosenenoken... prosepheren: offered... was offering — the two verb forms are significant: prosenenoken is perfect [has offered — the faith-act is completed in God's sight before the knife falls]; prosepheren is imperfect [was offering — the process was underway]): ton monogenē: the only-begotten/unique son — the same word as 11:17 and John 3:16 for Christ; the typological resonance between Isaac and Christ is built into the vocabulary. Ton tas epangelias anadexamenos: the one who had received the promises — the person who received the promise is the same one who offers the promise-bearer.
18: pros hon elalēthē hoti En Isaak klēthēsetai soi sperma — "even though God had said to him, It is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned" (Genesis 21:12): the conflict is explicit: God promised seed through Isaac, then commanded the offering of Isaac. En Isaak klēthēsetai soi sperma: in/through Isaac will be called to you seed — the prepositional en (in/through) indicates that Isaac is the channel of the promised lineage, not merely one of many options. The command to sacrifice Isaac is thus a direct apparent contradiction of the promise-mechanism. The faith of v17 consists precisely in proceeding despite this apparent contradiction.
19: logizomenos hoti kai ek nekrōn egeirein dynatos ho theos, hothen auton kai en parabolē ekomisato — "Abraham reasoned that God could even raise the dead, and so in a manner of speaking he did receive Isaac back from death" (logizomenos: reckoning, calculating, reasoning — the deliberate mental act of theological deduction): Abraham's faith is not blind but reasoned: if God can keep his promise only through Isaac, and God commands Isaac's death, then God must be able to raise the dead to fulfill the promise. En parabolē: in a parable, in a type — the recovery of Isaac is the type; the resurrection of Christ is the antitype. Isaac's survival of the intended death is a parabolic prefiguring of the resurrection.
20: Pistei kai peri mellontōn eulogēsen Isaak ton Iakōb kai ton Ēsau — "by faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau in regard to their future" (peri mellontōn: concerning things about to come, future things): Isaac's blessing of his sons was an act of faith because the blessings concerned things entirely beyond Isaac's power to guarantee. Eulogēsen: blessed — the patriarchal blessing was not mere wish but a faith-speech that committed God to the future of the blessed. The fact that the blessing held even when it was obtained through deception (Gen 27) is evidence of divine sovereignty over the means by which the promise advances.
21: Pistei Iakōb apothnēskōn hekaston tōn huiōn Iōsēph eulogēsen, kai prosekunēsen epi to akron tēs rhabdou autou — "by faith Jacob, when he was dying, blessed each of Joseph's sons, and worshipped as he leaned on the top of his staff" (Genesis 48:15-16; Hebrews 47:31): apothnēskōn: dying — the present participle; blessing on the threshold of death is the faith-act. The detail of leaning on the staff (from Genesis 47:31 LXX, where the MT has "head of the bed") is a translation difference; the LXX had rhabdon where the MT had mittah: the unpointed Hebrew allowed two readings. The posture of worship while dying is the faith-expression Hebrews highlights.
22: Pistei Iōsēph teleutōn peri tēs exodou tōn huiōn Israēl emnēmoneusen kai peri tōn ostōn autou eneteilato — "by faith Joseph, when he was dying, spoke of the exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions about his bones" (Genesis 50:24-25): tēs exodou: the exodus — the same word Luke uses for Jesus's departure in the Transfiguration (Luke 9:31). Joseph's faith-act is prophetic: dying in Egypt, he spoke of a future departure from Egypt and committed his bones to go with the departing people. Peri tōn ostōn autou eneteilato: gave instructions about his bones — Genesis 50:25, the oath Moses honored in Exodus 13:19. The bones left Egypt as a sign that Joseph's faith reached beyond his own death to the promised inheritance.
23: Pistei Mōysēs gennētheis ekrybē trimēnon hypo tōn paterōn autou, dioti eidon asteion to paidion kai ouk ephobēthēsan to diatagma tou basileōs — "by faith Moses' parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was a fine child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict" (asteion to paidion: the child was fine/beautiful — asteios: graceful, well-formed; Acts 7:20 uses asteios tō theō, beautiful to God): the faith-act is the parents', not Moses's — they saw something in the child and acted on it in defiance of royal decree. Ouk ephobēthēsan to diatagma tou basileōs: they were not afraid of the king's edict — the same structure as Moses's non-fear of the king's anger in v27.
25: mallon helomenos synkakouchesthai tō laō tou theou ē proskairon echein hamartias apolausin — "he chose to be mistreated along with the people of God rather than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin" (helomenos: having chosen — aorist middle participle of haireō: to take for oneself, to choose deliberately; the middle voice emphasizes the personal, self-involving character of the choice): proskairon: temporary, for a season — pros kairon: towards/for a time; the pleasures of sin are characterized by their temporality. Apolausin: enjoyment, pleasure — the Epicurean vocabulary of present pleasure; Moses's calculation reverses it: temporary pleasure vs. lasting reward.
27: Pistei katelipen Aigupton mē phobētheis ton thymon tou basileōs, ton gar aoroton hōs horōn ekarteresen — "by faith he left Egypt, not fearing the king's anger; he persevered because he saw him who is invisible" (ton aoroton hōs horōn: the invisible one as seeing — the paradox of faith-sight: seeing what cannot be seen physically): aoroton: the unseen, invisible one — the divine invisibility is not absence but the nature of God's being. Ekarteresen: endured, persevered — kartereō: to be strong, to hold one's ground (from kratos = strength); the endurance is strength-based, not mere resignation. Moses held his position because his sight was fixed on the one who cannot be seen by the eyes.
28: Pistei pepoiēken to pascha kai tēn proschusin tou haimatos, hina mē ho olothreuōn ta prōtotoka thigē autōn — "by faith he kept the Passover and the application of blood, so that the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch them" (pepoiēken: has kept/made — perfect active: the faith-act stands permanently): proschusin tou haimatos: the pouring/sprinkling on of blood — prochysis: a pouring forth; the application of the Passover blood to the doorposts. Ho olothreuōn: the destroyer — the destroying angel of Exodus 12:23; thigē: might touch — the aorst subjunctive of thinganō: to touch, to make contact. The blood-application is the faith-act that created the boundary the destroyer could not cross.
29: Pistei diebēsan tēn Erythrān Thalassan hōs dia xēras gēs, hēs perian labontes hoi Aigyptioi katepothēsan — "by faith the people passed through the Red Sea as on dry land; but when the Egyptians tried to do so, they were drowned" (diebēsan: passed through — aorist of diabainō: to cross over, to traverse): the same water that was dry land for Israel was drowning water for Egypt — the same reality, differentiated by faith. Hōs dia xēras gēs (as through dry land): the hōs (as, like) — as if it were dry land, though it was sea-bottom. Katepothēsan: were swallowed up — katapinō: to drink down, to swallow completely; the passive: the sea swallowed the Egyptians who pursued on the same basis.
30: Pistei ta teichē Ierichō epeson kyklōthenta epi hepta hēmeras — "by faith the walls of Jericho fell, after the army had marched around them for seven days" (epeson: fell — aorist; kyklōthenta: having been circled — aorist passive participle of kykloō: to encircle): the faith-logic: seven days of marching around walls achieves nothing militarily — the walls fell not by siege-craft but by obedience to a divine command whose rationality was not self-evident. The connection between the commanded action and the result was faith alone.
31: Pistei Raab hē pornē ou synap ōleto tois apeitēsasin, dexamēnē tous kataskopous met' eirēnēs — "by faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient, because she welcomed the spies in peace" (hē pornē: the prostitute — the social status is retained to heighten the faith-contrast: the Canaanite prostitute believed what Israel had heard of YHWH and acted on it): tois apeitēsasin: those who were disobedient — the same apeithia-root as the wilderness generation; the Jericho population's failure is categorized as disobedience, implying they had sufficient information to act differently. Met' eirēnēs: in peace — her welcome was not coerced but peaceful, establishing covenant-like solidarity.
32: kai ti eti legō; epileipsei gar me diēgoumenon ho chronos peri Gedeon, Barak, Sampson, Iephthae, Dauid te kai Samouēl kai tōn prophētōn — "and what more shall I say? Time would fail me to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets" (epileipsei gar me diēgoumenon ho chronos: time will fail me as I narrate — the rhetorical device of paralipsis / praeteritio: listing what one says cannot be listed): the list order is not strictly chronological (Barak precedes Samson, but David follows Jephthah); the rhetorical move is cumulative impression, not historical sequence. Diēgoumenon: narrating — the author is aware of his own discourse, breaking the narrative frame to speak about the act of narrating.
34: esbesan dynamin pyros, ephygon stomata machairēs, enedunamōthēsan apo astheneias, egenethesan ischyroi en polemō, parembolas eklinan allotriōn — "who quenched the fury of the flames, and escaped the edge of the sword; whose weakness was turned to strength; and who became powerful in battle and routed foreign armies" (esbesan dynamin pyros: quenched the power of fire — the three Hebrew youths in the furnace [Dan 3]; dynamin pyros: the power of fire, not merely the fire itself): enedunamōthēsan apo astheneias: were strengthened out of weakness — apo: the weakness was the starting point from which the strengthening emerged. Parembolas eklinan allotriōn: they caused the camps of foreigners to turn, to flee — eklinō: to bend, to turn from.
36: heteroi de empaigmōn kai mastigōn perian labontes, eti de desmaōn kai phylakēs — "some faced jeers and flogging, and even chains and imprisonment" (empaigmōn: of mockings, derisions — from empaizō: to mock, to play-act at someone's expense; the same word for the mocking of Jesus in the passion narratives): the two tiers of suffering are noted: those who received miraculous deliverance (vv32-35) and those who suffered without physical rescue (vv36-38). Both categories are commended equally by faith. The structure prevents a prosperity-faith reading: faith does not guarantee miraculous rescue.
37: elethasthēsan, epristvthēsan, en phonō machairēs apethanon, periēlthon en mēlōtais, en aigeiais dermasin, hysteroumenoi, thlibomenoi, kakouchoumenoi — "they were put to death by stoning; they were sawn in two; they were killed by the sword. They went about in sheepskins and goatskins, destitute, persecuted and mistreated" (epristvthēsan: they were sawn in two — traditionally applied to Isaiah [not in the OT text, but in Jewish tradition recorded in Lives of the Prophets]): en mēlōtais, en aigeiais dermasin: in sheepskins, in goat-skins — the covering of prophets, Elijah's mantle (1 Kings 19:13); the clothing marks them as outsiders to civilization. Hysteroumenoi, thlibomenoi, kakouchoumenoi: lacking, pressed, maltreated — three progressive participles of suffering.
38: hōn ouk ēn axios ho kosmos, epi erēmiais planōmenoi kai oresin kai spēlaiois kai tais opais tēs gēs — "the world was not worthy of them. They wandered in deserts and mountains, and in caves and holes in the ground" (hōn ouk ēn axios ho kosmos: of whom the world was not worthy): the evaluative reversal: from the world's perspective they were marginal figures, driven out and hiding; from God's perspective they were too good for the world that rejected them. Epi erēmiais planōmenoi: wandering in desolate places — the irony is that those who sought the heavenly city were reduced to inhabiting caves and holes; the dispossession confirms the reality of their citizenship elsewhere.
40: tou theou peri hēmōn kreition ti problepsamenos, hina mē chōris hēmōn teleiōthōsin — "God had planned something better for us so that only together with us would they be made perfect" (problepsamenos: having foreseen and provided for beforehand — pro: before + blepō: to see; the divine foreseeing that shapes the historical arrangement): kreition ti: something better — the better thing is the completed Christ-event that the OT saints anticipated but did not receive. Hina mē chōris hēmōn teleiōthōsin: so that apart from us they would not be made perfect — the perfection is corporate: the new covenant community is the completion-context for OT faith. The cloud of witnesses cannot receive their perfection without the community for whom Christ has come.
